title.zh: 卷九十六上 西域傳
title.en: Volume 96a: Traditions of the Western Regions 1
title.raw: 卷九十六上 西域傳 Volume 96a: Traditions of the Western Regions 1

sentences.zh: 卷九十六上 西域傳第六十六上

translations.literal: Volume 96a: Traditions of the Western Regions, Part One
translations.idiomatic: Book of Han, scroll 96a: Traditions of the Western Regions (part one)

sentences.zh: 西域以孝武時始通，本三十六國，其後稍分至五十餘，皆在匈奴之西，烏孫之南。

translations.literal: The Western Regions first opened communication in Emperor Xiaowu’s time; originally thirty-six states; afterward gradually divided to more than fifty—all lay west of the Xiongnu and south of Wusun.
translations.idiomatic: Contact began under Emperor Wu; thirty-six original polities later split into fifty-odd, all west of the Xiongnu and south of Wusun.

sentences.zh: 南北有大山，中央有河，東西六千餘里，南北千餘里。

translations.literal: North and south have great mountains; in the center is a river; east–west more than six thousand li, north–south more than one thousand li.
translations.idiomatic: High ranges north and south bracket a central river plain—more than six thousand li east to west and a thousand north to south.

sentences.zh: 東則接漢，厄以玉門、陽關，西則限以蔥嶺。

translations.literal: East it adjoins Han, choked by Yumen and Yang Passes; west it is bounded by the Congling ranges.
translations.idiomatic: Yumen and Yangguan pinch the eastern edge toward Han; the Pamirs wall it off on the west.

sentences.zh: 其南山，東出金城，與漢南山屬焉。

translations.literal: Its southern mountains exit east at Jincheng and join Han’s southern mountains.
translations.idiomatic: The southern range runs east through Jincheng and links to Han’s Qinling spine.

sentences.zh: 其河有兩原：一出蔥嶺出，一出于闐。

translations.literal: Its river has two sources: one emerges from the Congling; one emerges from Khotan.
translations.idiomatic: The river has twin headwaters—one from the Pamirs, one from Khotan.

sentences.zh: 于闐在南山下，其河北流，與蔥嶺河合，東注蒲昌海。

translations.literal: Khotan lies below the southern mountains; its river flows north, joins the Congling river, and pours east into Lake Puchang.
translations.idiomatic: South of the range, Khotan’s stream runs north to merge with the Pamir-fed branch and drain east into Lop Nur.

sentences.zh: 蒲昌海，一名鹽澤者也，去玉門、陽關三百餘里，廣袤三四百里。

translations.literal: Lake Puchang—also called the Salt Marsh—lies more than three hundred li from Yumen and Yang passes; its breadth and length are three or four hundred li.
translations.idiomatic: Puchang—also the Salt Marsh—sits three hundred li beyond the frontier passes and spans three or four hundred li.

sentences.zh: 其水亭居，冬夏不增減，皆以為潛行地下，南出於積石，為中國河雲。

translations.literal: Its waters stand level; winter and summer they neither increase nor decrease—all deem them to run hidden underground, emerging south at Jishi, becoming the Central States’ Yellow River, it is said.
translations.idiomatic: The lake neither rises nor falls with the seasons—many believed it flowed under the desert to surface as the Yellow River at Jishi.

sentences.zh: 自玉門、陽關出西域有兩道：從鄯善傍南山北，波河西行至莎車，為南道，南道西逾蔥嶺則出大月氏、安息。

translations.literal: From Yumen and Yang passes into the Western Regions there are two routes: from Shanshan along the southern mountains’ north, following the river west to Kashgar—this is the southern route; west of the southern route crossing Congling leads out to Great Yuezhi and Parthia.
translations.idiomatic: Two roads led west from the passes: the southern ran from Shanshan along the Kunlun flank to Kashgar, then over the Pamirs to Yuezhi and Parthia.

sentences.zh: 自車師前王廷隨北山，波河西行至疏勒，為北道，北道西逾蔥嶺則出大宛、康居、奄蔡焉。

translations.literal: From Jushi anterior royal court following the northern mountains, following the river west to Shule—this is the northern route; west of the northern route crossing Congling leads out to Ferghana, Kangju, and Aorsi.
translations.idiomatic: The northern ran from Turfan along the Tianshan to Kashgar, then over the Pamirs to Ferghana, Kangju, and the steppe confederation beyond.

sentences.zh: 西域諸國大率土著，有城郭田畜，與匈奴、烏孫異俗，故皆役屬匈奴。

translations.literal: Western Regions states were largely native sedentary peoples with walls, fields, and herds—customs unlike Xiongnu and Wusun—therefore all were conscript-tributary to the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: Most realms were oasis farmers behind walls—nothing like the nomads—so the Xiongnu kept them in tributary harness.

sentences.zh: 匈奴西邊日逐王置僮僕都尉，使領西域，常居焉耆、危須、尉黎間，賦稅諸國，取富給焉。

translations.literal: The Xiongnu western frontier’s Pursuit-of-the-Sun king established the Bondsman Commandant, ordering him to oversee the Western Regions; he constantly dwelt between Yanqi, Weixu, and Weili, levying taxes on the states and drawing wealth and sufficiency.
translations.idiomatic: The western Xiongnu prince posted a “bondservant commandant” between Yanqi, Weixu, and Korla to skim tribute.

sentences.zh: 自周衰，戎狄錯居涇渭之北。

translations.literal: From Zhou’s decline, Rong and Di intermingled dwelling north of Jing and Wei.
translations.idiomatic: After Zhou’s fall, Di and Rong peoples dotted the country north of the Jing and Wei.

sentences.zh: 及秦始皇攘卻戎狄，築長城，界中國，然西不過臨洮。

translations.literal: When First Emperor of Qin drove back Rong and Di, built the Long Wall, and bordered the Central States, yet west did not pass Lintao.
translations.idiomatic: The First Emperor’s wall boxed “China” in—but its western end stopped at Lintao.

sentences.zh: 漢興至於孝武，事征四夷，廣威德，而張騫始開西域之跡。

translations.literal: From Han’s rise down to Emperor Xiaowu, undertaking campaigns against the four Yi, broadening awe and virtue—Zhang Qian first opened traces into the Western Regions.
translations.idiomatic: From Gaozu through Emperor Wu’s frontier wars, Zhang Qian first traced a road into the west.

sentences.zh: 其後驃騎將軍擊破匈奴右地，降渾邪、休屠王，遂空其地，始築令居以西，初置酒泉郡，後稍發徙民充實之，分置武威、張掖、敦煌，列四郡，據兩關焉。

translations.literal: Afterward Cavalry-General smashed Xiongnu right territory, submitting Kings Huntun and Xiutu—thereupon emptying that land—first built west of Lingju, first placing Jiuquan commandery; afterward gradually mobilizing relocated commoners to fill it, divided placing Wuwei, Zhangye, Dunhuang—arraying four commanderies, holding two passes.
translations.idiomatic: Huo Qubing’s victory emptied Hexi; Han walled west from Lingju, planted Jiuquan, then Wuwei, Zhangye, and Dunhuang—the four western commanderies anchoring the twin passes.

sentences.zh: 自貳師將軍伐大宛之後，西域震懼，多遣使來貢獻。

translations.literal: From General of Ershi campaigning against Ferghana afterward, the Western Regions trembled in fear; many dispatched envoys coming with tribute.
translations.idiomatic: Li Guangli’s Ferghana expedition terrified the oases—embassies and gifts multiplied.

sentences.zh: 漢使西域者益得職。

translations.literal: Han envoys to the Western Regions increasingly fulfilled duty.
translations.idiomatic: Han missions west gained real traction.

sentences.zh: 於是自敦煌西至鹽澤，往往起亭，而輪台、渠犁皆有田卒數百人，置使者校尉領護，以給使外國者。

translations.literal: Thereupon from Dunhuang west to Salt Marsh watchtowers arose everywhere; moreover Yulongtai and Karashahr each had several hundred garrison-farm troops—Envoy-Colonel placed to lead protection—to supply those serving abroad.
translations.idiomatic: Watchtowers ran from Dunhuang to Lop Nur; hundreds of farmers garrisoned Luntai and Karashahr under an Envoy-Colonel for outbound missions.

sentences.zh: 至宣帝時，遣衛司馬使護鄯善以西數國。

translations.literal: Down to Emperor Xuandi’s time, he dispatched Guards Major to protect several states west of Shanshan.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Xuan, a Guards Major was posted to shield the kingdoms west of Shanshan.

sentences.zh: 及破姑師，未盡殄，分以為車師前後王及山北六國。

translations.literal: When they broke Jushi, not wholly exterminated—divided it into Jushi anterior and posterior kings and six states north of the mountains.
translations.idiomatic: Jushi’s fall split the land into anterior and posterior Turfan kings plus six northern rim polities.

sentences.zh: 時漢獨護南道，未能盡並北道也。

translations.literal: At the time Han solely protected the southern route; could not wholly annex the northern route.
translations.idiomatic: Han held only the southern road—the northern remained contested.

sentences.zh: 然匈奴不自安矣。

translations.literal: Yet the Xiongnu were no longer at ease.
translations.idiomatic: The Xiongnu grew uneasy.

sentences.zh: 其後日逐王畔單于，將眾來降，護鄯善以西使者鄭吉迎之。

translations.literal: Afterward Pursuit-of-the-Sun king rebelled against the Chanyu, leading his host to come surrender; envoy guarding west of Shanshan Zheng Ji welcomed them.
translations.idiomatic: When the western prince broke from the Chanyu and surrendered, Zheng Ji—the envoy west of Shanshan—received him.

sentences.zh: 既至漢，封日逐王為歸德侯，吉為安遠侯。

translations.literal: Having reached Han, Pursuit-of-the-Sun king was enfeoffed Marquis of Guide; Ji was enfeoffed Marquis of Pacifying-the-Distant.
translations.idiomatic: The Son of Heaven made the prince Marquis of Guide and Zheng Ji Marquis of Pacifying-the-Distant.

sentences.zh: 是歲，神爵二年也。

translations.literal: This year was Shenjue second year.
translations.idiomatic: That was Shenjue 2.

sentences.zh: 乃因使吉並護北道，故號曰都護。

translations.literal: Thereupon by appointment made Ji jointly protect the northern route—therefore titled Protector-General.
translations.idiomatic: Ji was ordered to oversee both routes—hence the title Protector-General.

sentences.zh: 都護之起，自吉置矣。

translations.literal: Protector-General’s rise—from Ji’s establishment.
translations.idiomatic: The office began with Zheng Ji.

sentences.zh: 僮僕都尉由此罷，匈奴益弱，不得近西域。

translations.literal: Bondsman Commandant thereby abolished; Xiongnu grew weaker; could not approach the Western Regions.
translations.idiomatic: The bondservant tax post vanished—Han blocked Xiongnu access to the Tarim.

sentences.zh: 於是徙屯田，田於北胥鞬，披莎車之地，屯田校尉始屬都護。

translations.literal: Thereupon shifted garrison-farm; farmed at Beixujian; opened Kashgar lands—garrison-farm colonel first subordinated to Protector-General.
translations.idiomatic: Han pushed farms to Beixujian and sliced into Kashgar—the colonel of garrison troops now answered to the Protector-General.

sentences.zh: 都護督察烏孫、康居諸外國，動靜有變以聞。

translations.literal: Protector-General supervised and inspected Wusun, Kangju, and various outer states—if movement and stillness had change, reported upward.
translations.idiomatic: He monitored Wusun, Kangju, and beyond—any stir required memorial.

sentences.zh: 可安輯，安輯之；

translations.literal: If they could be settled and gathered—settle and gather them;
translations.idiomatic: Pacify where pacification worked;

sentences.zh: 可擊，擊之。

translations.literal: if they could be struck—strike them.
translations.idiomatic: strike where force was needed.

sentences.zh: 都護治烏壘城，去陽關二千七百三十八里，與渠犁田官相近，土地肥饒，於西域為中，故都護治焉。

translations.literal: Protector-General governed from Wulei city, 2738 li from Yang Pass, near Karashahr farm officials—land fertile—among Western Regions central—therefore Protector-General governs there.
translations.idiomatic: The headquarters sat at Wulei—2738 li west of Yangguan—near Karashahr’s farms in the Tarim’s fertile middle.

sentences.zh: 至元帝時，復置戊己校尉，屯田車師前王庭。

translations.literal: Down to Emperor Yuandi’s time, again placed Wu–Ji Colonel, garrison-farming at Jushi anterior royal court.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Yuan restored the Wu–Ji colonel and planted farms at Turfan’s anterior court.

sentences.zh: 是時，匈奴東蒲類王茲力支將人眾千七百餘人降都護，都護分車師後王之西為烏貪訾離地以處之。

translations.literal: At this time Xiongnu eastern King of Pulei Zilizhi led a host more than seventeen hundred to surrender to Protector-General; Protector-General divided west of Jushi posterior king as Wuchen-zili lands to settle them.
translations.idiomatic: When eastern Pulei prince Zilizhi brought seventeen hundred followers in, the Protector-General carved Wuchen-zili pasture west of posterior Turfan for their settlement.

sentences.zh: 自宣、元後，單于稱籓臣，西域服從。

translations.literal: From Xuandi and Yuandi onward, Chanyu styled himself frontier vassal; Western Regions submitted in obedience.
translations.idiomatic: After Xuan and Yuan, the Chanyu accepted vassal rank and the Tarim fell in line.

sentences.zh: 其土地山川、王侯戶數、道裡遠近，翔實矣。

translations.literal: Its lands and mountains and rivers, kings and marquises’ household counts, road mileage far and near—full and detailed.
translations.idiomatic: Territory, demographics, and distances could finally be recorded in detail.

sentences.zh: 出陽關，自近者始，曰婼羌。

translations.literal: Exiting Yang Pass, beginning from the nearest—called Ruoqiang.
translations.idiomatic: West of Yangguan the first realm is Ruoqiang.

sentences.zh: 婼羌國王號去胡來王。

translations.literal: Ruoqiang state’s king is titled King Who Left the Hu and Came.
translations.idiomatic: Its king bore the title “He Who Left the Hu and Came.”

sentences.zh: 去陽關千八百里，去長安六千三百里，辟在西南，不當孔道。

translations.literal: 1800 li from Yang Pass, 6300 li from Chang’an—remote in southwest—not on the main thoroughfare.
translations.idiomatic: Eighteen hundred li from Yangguan, sixty-three hundred from Chang’an—it sits off the main road in the southwest.

sentences.zh: 戶四百五十，口千七百五十，勝兵者五百人。

translations.literal: 450 households, 1750 mouths, 500 battle-ready.
translations.idiomatic: Four hundred fifty households, seventeen hundred fifty people, five hundred fighters.

sentences.zh: 西與且末接。

translations.literal: West adjoins Qiemo.
translations.idiomatic: Qiemo lies to its west.

sentences.zh: 隨畜逐不草，不田作，仰鄯善、且末谷。

translations.literal: Following livestock chasing whatever pasture—no farming—relying on Shanshan and Qiemo grain.
translations.idiomatic: They followed herds after sparse grazing, farmed nothing, and bought grain from Shanshan and Qiemo.

sentences.zh: 山有鐵，自作兵，後有弓、矛、服刀、劍、甲。

translations.literal: Mountains have iron; they make weapons themselves—later having bow, spear, waist knife, sword, armor.
translations.idiomatic: Mountain iron fed local forges—bows, spears, belt knives, swords, and armor.

sentences.zh: 西北至鄯善，乃當道雲。

translations.literal: Northwest to Shanshan—then on the main route, it is said.
translations.idiomatic: Northwest lies Shanshan—where the highway runs.

sentences.zh: 鄯善國，本名樓蘭，王治扞泥城，去陽關千六百里，去長安六千一百里。

translations.literal: Shanshan state—originally named Loulan—king rules from Kroraina city; 1600 li from Yang Pass, 6100 li from Chang’an.
translations.idiomatic: Shanshan—once Loulan—ruled from Kroraina: sixteen hundred li west of Yangguan, sixty-one hundred from Chang’an.

sentences.zh: 戶千五百七十，口萬四千一百，勝兵二千九百十二人。

translations.literal: 1570 households, 14,100 mouths, 2912 battle-ready.
translations.idiomatic: Fifteen hundred seventy households, fourteen thousand one hundred people, twenty-nine hundred twelve troops.

sentences.zh: 輔國侯、卻胡侯、鄯善都尉、擊車師都尉、左右且渠、擊車師君各一人，譯長二人。

translations.literal: State-supporting marquis, Hu-repelling marquis, Shanshan commandant, Jushi-striking commandant, left and right qütu, Jushi-striking lord—each one person; chief interpreters two.
translations.idiomatic: One state-support marquis, one Hu-repeller, Shanshan commandant, Turfan-strike commandant, paired qütu chiefs, Turfan-strike lord—and two chief interpreters.

sentences.zh: 西北去都護治所千七百八十五里，至墨山國千三百六十五里，西北至車師千八百九十里。

translations.literal: Northwest from Protector-General seat 1785 li; to Ink Mountain state 1365 li; northwest to Jushi 1890 li.
translations.idiomatic: 1785 li northwest of Wulei; 1365 to Karasahr’s mountains; 1890 northwest to Turfan.

sentences.zh: 地沙鹵，少田，寄田仰谷旁國。

translations.literal: Land sandy-briny, little cropland—borrow-fields relying on grain from neighboring states.
translations.idiomatic: Saline sands offered little farmland—they leased fields and grain from neighbors.

sentences.zh: 國出玉，多葭葦、檉柳、胡桐、白草。

translations.literal: State produces jade; much reed-rush, tamarisk, diversiform poplar, white grass.
translations.idiomatic: The kingdom yielded jade plus reeds, tamarisk, poplar, and pale steppe grass.

sentences.zh: 民隨率牧逐水草，有驢馬，多橐它。

translations.literal: People follow pasture officers chasing water and grass—have donkeys and horses; many camels.
translations.idiomatic: Herders followed officers to pasture—donkeys, horses, and many camels.

sentences.zh: 能作兵，與婼羌同。

translations.literal: Able to make weapons—same as Ruoqiang.
translations.idiomatic: Their arms matched Ruoqiang’s craft.

sentences.zh: 初，武帝鹹張騫之言，甘心欲通大宛諸國，使者相望於道，一歲中多至十餘輩。

translations.literal: At first Emperor Wu all believed Zhang Qian’s words—heart willingly wished to open Ferghana and various states—envoys sighting one another on the road—in one year often reaching more than ten parties.
translations.idiomatic: Wu-di trusted Zhang Qian’s maps and craved the Ferghana road—caravans of envoys passed each other, a dozen missions some years.

sentences.zh: 樓蘭、姑師當道，苦之，攻劫漢使王恢等，又數為匈奴耳目，令其兵遮漢使。

translations.literal: Loulan and Jushi blocked the road—suffered from them—they attacked and robbed Han envoys Wang Hui and others; moreover repeatedly acted as Xiongnu eyes and ears, ordering their troops intercept Han envoys.
translations.idiomatic: Loulan and Turfan straddled the highway—they raided missions like Wang Hui’s and spied for the Xiongnu, blocking Han traffic.

sentences.zh: 漢使多言其國有城邑，兵弱易擊。

translations.literal: Han envoys largely said their states had walled towns—troops weak—easy to strike.
translations.idiomatic: Han reports painted them as walled but weak—easy prey.

sentences.zh: 於是武帝遣從票侯趙破奴將屬國騎及郡兵數萬擊姑師。

translations.literal: Thereupon Emperor Wu dispatched Follow-the-Banner Marquis Zhao Ponu leading dependent-state cavalry and commandery troops numbering tens of thousands to strike Jushi.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor sent Zhao Ponu with tens of thousands of allied cavalry and county troops against Turfan.

sentences.zh: 王恢數為樓蘭所苦，上令恢佐破奴將兵。

translations.literal: Wang Hui repeatedly suffered from Loulan—the emperor ordered Hui assist Ponu commanding troops.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Hui—often ambushed by Loulan—was ordered to serve under Zhao Ponu.

sentences.zh: 破奴與輕騎七百人先至，虜樓蘭王遂破姑師，因暴兵威以動烏孫、大宛之屬。

translations.literal: Ponu with light cavalry seven hundred first arrived—captured Loulan king—thereupon broke Jushi—thereby displaying martial awe to move Wusun, Ferghana, and kind.
translations.idiomatic: Ponu raced ahead with seven hundred horse—bagged the Loulan king, smashed Turfan, and flashed steel at Wusun and Ferghana.

sentences.zh: 還，封破奴為浞野侯，恢為浩侯。

translations.literal: Returning, Ponu was enfeoffed Marquis of Zhuoye; Hui Marquis of Hao.
translations.idiomatic: Homecoming brought Zhao Ponu the Zhuoye marquisate and Wang Hui the Hao marquisate.

sentences.zh: 於是漢列亭障至玉門矣。

translations.literal: Thereupon Han arrayed watchtowers and barriers to Yumen.
translations.idiomatic: Han then ran watchtowers and walls clear to Yumen.

sentences.zh: 樓蘭既降服貢獻，匈奴聞，發兵擊之。

translations.literal: Loulan having submitted and brought tribute, Xiongnu heard and raised troops striking them.
translations.idiomatic: Loulan’s submission drew a Xiongnu punitive strike.

sentences.zh: 於是樓蘭遣一子質匈奴，一子質漢。

translations.literal: Thereupon Loulan dispatched one son as hostage to Xiongnu, one son hostage to Han.
translations.idiomatic: Loulan parked one prince in Han and one with the Chanyu.

sentences.zh: 後貳師軍擊大宛，匈奴欲遮之，貳師兵盛不敢當，即遣騎因樓蘭候漢使後過者，欲絕勿通。

translations.literal: Later Ershi army struck Ferghana; Xiongnu wished to intercept—Ershi troops mighty—did not dare withstand—immediately dispatched cavalry via Loulan waiting on Han envoys passing afterward—wishing to cut off and not permit passage.
translations.idiomatic: During Li Guangli’s Ferghana campaign the Xiongnu dared not face his host—instead they used Loulan riders to ambush straggling Han missions.

sentences.zh: 時漢軍正任文將兵屯玉門關，為貳師後距，捕得生口，知狀以聞。

translations.literal: At the time Han Colonel Ren Wen commanded troops garrisoned Yumen Pass—as Ershi’s rear guard—captured living tongue—learned situation and reported upward.
translations.idiomatic: Colonel Ren Wen at Yumen captured prisoners and reported the plot.

sentences.zh: 上詔文便道引兵捕樓蘭王。

translations.literal: The emperor edicted Wen by convenient route lead troops capture Loulan king.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor ordered Ren Wen to seize the Loulan king by detachment.

sentences.zh: 將指闕，簿責王，對曰：「小國在大國間，不兩屬無以自安。

translations.literal: Bringing him pointing toward palace gate, ledger-rebuked king—answered saying: ‘Small state between great states—without dual attachment cannot secure itself.’
translations.idiomatic: Dragged to Chang’an for interrogation, the king said: ‘A small realm caught between giants cannot survive without serving both.’

sentences.zh: 願徙國入居漢地。

translations.literal: I wish to move the state entering to dwell in Han lands.’
translations.idiomatic: Let us relocate inside Han borders.’

sentences.zh: 」上直其言，遣歸國，亦因使候司匈奴。

translations.literal: ’ The emperor deemed his words upright, sent him back to state—also thereby ordered him spy on Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: ’ The emperor accepted the plea—sent him home as a Han agent on the steppe.

sentences.zh: 匈奴自是不甚親信樓蘭。

translations.literal: Xiongnu from this not greatly intimate trusted Loulan.
translations.idiomatic: The Chanyu thereafter kept Loulan at arm’s length.

sentences.zh: 征和元年，樓蘭王死，國人來請質子在漢者，欲立之。

translations.literal: Zhenghe first year Loulan king died; countrymen came requesting crown prince in Han—wished to establish him.
translations.idiomatic: In Zhenghe 1 the king died; Loulan asked for their prince held hostage in Han.

sentences.zh: 質子常坐漢法，下蠶室宮刑，故不遣。

translations.literal: Crown prince had been condemned under Han law—sent to silkworm chamber for palace mutilation—therefore not dispatched.
translations.idiomatic: That prince had been castrated under Han law—so Han refused release.

sentences.zh: 報曰：「侍子，天子愛之，不能遣。

translations.literal: Reply said: ‘Attendant son—Son of Heaven cherishes him—cannot dispatch.’
translations.idiomatic: Han answered: ‘The emperor favors your hostage—he stays.’

sentences.zh: 其更立其次當立者。

translations.literal: Further establish the next one fit to be established.’
translations.idiomatic: Enthrone the next eligible heir.’

sentences.zh: 」樓蘭更立王，漢復責其質子，亦遣一子質匈奴。

translations.literal: ’ Loulan changed establishing king; Han again demanded his hostage—also dispatched one son hostage to Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: ’ Loulan crowned another king—Han demanded a new hostage while Loulan again sent a prince north.

sentences.zh: 後王又死，匈奴先聞之，遣質子歸，得立為王。

translations.literal: Later king again died; Xiongnu first heard—dispatched hostage returning—obtained establishment as king.
translations.idiomatic: When the next king died, the Xiongnu rushed their hostage home first—so he took the throne.

sentences.zh: 漢遣使詔新王，令入朝，天子將加厚賞。

translations.literal: Han dispatched envoy edicting new king—ordering enter court audience—Son of Heaven about to add thick rewards.
translations.idiomatic: Han summoned the new king to Chang’an with promises of rich gifts.

sentences.zh: 樓蘭王后妻，故繼母也，謂王曰：「先王遣兩子質漢皆不還，奈何欲往朝乎？

translations.literal: Loulan king’s later wife—formerly stepmother—said to king: ‘Former king dispatched two sons hostage to Han—both not returned—how wish to go court audience?’
translations.idiomatic: The king’s wife—once his stepmother—warned: ‘Your father sent two sons to Han—neither returned—why court suicide?’

sentences.zh: 」王用其計，謝使曰：「新立，國未定，願待後年入見天子。

translations.literal: ’ King used her plan—declined envoy saying: ‘Newly established—state not settled—wish wait later year enter see Son of Heaven.’
translations.idiomatic: ’ He stalled the envoy: ‘The realm is unsettled—I will attend next year.’

sentences.zh: 」然樓蘭國最在東垂，近漢，當白龍堆，乏水草，常主發導，負水儋糧，送迎漢使，又數為吏卒所寇，懲艾不便與漢通。

translations.literal: ’ Yet Loulan state utmost on eastern rim—near Han—faces White Dragon Mounds—lacks water grass—constantly presides dispatching guides—bearing water shouldering grain—seeing off welcoming Han envoys—moreover repeatedly plundered by clerks and soldiers—punished and cut—finds inconvenient communicating with Han.
translations.idiomatic: ’ Yet Loulan lay farthest east—hard by the White Dragon dunes—where Han escorts drained water and grain while rude soldiers looted—so the court soured on Han traffic.

sentences.zh: 後復為匈奴後間，數遮殺漢使。

translations.literal: Later again became Xiongnu rear spy—repeatedly intercepted killing Han envoys.
translations.idiomatic: Later they again served the Xiongnu—ambushing Han envoys.

sentences.zh: 其弟尉屠耆降漢，具言狀。

translations.literal: His younger brother Weituqi surrendered to Han—fully stated situation.
translations.idiomatic: Prince Weituqi defected and told all.

sentences.zh: 元鳳四年，大將軍霍光白遣平樂監傅介子往刺其王。

translations.literal: Yuanfeng fourth year, Grand General Huo Guang memorialized dispatching Pingle intendant Fu Jiezi to go assassinate their king.
translations.idiomatic: In Yuanfeng 4 Huo Guang proposed Fu Jiezi’s embassy-assassination.

sentences.zh: 介子輕將勇敢士，繼金幣，揚言以賜外國為名。

translations.literal: Jiezi lightly led brave stalwarts, bearing gold coin—openly declaring bestowing outer states as pretext.
translations.idiomatic: Fu Jiezi took picked swordsmen and treasure chests under cover of imperial gifts.

sentences.zh: 既至樓蘭，詐其王欲賜之，王喜，與介子飲，醉，將其王屏語，壯士二人從後刺殺之，貴人左右皆散走。

translations.literal: Having reached Loulan—deceived their king wishing to gift him—king pleased—drank with Jiezi—drunk—led his king screen aside speaking—two stalwarts from behind stab-killed him—nobles left and right all scattered fleeing.
translations.idiomatic: He baited the king with gifts, drank him blind, drew him aside—and two blades struck from behind while retainers fled.

sentences.zh: 介子告諭以：「王負漢罪，天子遣我誅王，當更立王弟尉屠耆在漢者。

translations.literal: Jiezi announced instructing: ‘King betrayed Han guilt—Son of Heaven dispatched me execute king—ought further establish king’s younger brother Weituqi who is in Han.’
translations.idiomatic: Fu Jiezi proclaimed: ‘Your king offended Han—the Son of Heaven sent me to kill him; Weituqi in Chang’an will succeed.’

sentences.zh: 漢兵方至，毋敢動，自令滅國矣！

translations.literal: Han troops about to arrive—do not dare move—or yourselves invite destroying the state!’
translations.idiomatic: Han troops are coming—stir and you erase your kingdom!’

sentences.zh: 」介子遂斬王嘗歸首，馳傳詣闕，懸首北闕下。

translations.literal: ’ Jiezi thereupon beheaded king Changgui’s head—galloped relay to palace gate—suspended head below north gate.
translations.idiomatic: ’ He sent the king’s head to Chang’an by post-rider and spiked it below the north gate.

sentences.zh: 封介子為義陽侯。

translations.literal: He enfeoffed Jiezi Marquis of Yiyang.
translations.idiomatic: Fu Jiezi became Marquis of Yiyang.

sentences.zh: 乃立尉屠耆為王，更名其國為鄯善，為刻印章，賜以宮女為夫人，備車騎輜重，丞相將軍率百官送至橫門外，祖而遣之。

translations.literal: Thereupon established Weituqi as king—renamed their state Shanshan—carved seal—bestowed palace woman as consort—prepared chariots cavalry baggage train—chancellor general led hundred officials seeing off beyond Horizontal Gate—libation send-off dispatching him.
translations.idiomatic: Weituqi became king of renamed Shanshan—with new seal, a Han consort, equipage, and a state farewell beyond the Horizontal Gate.

sentences.zh: 王自請天子曰：「身在漢久，今歸，單弱，而前王有子在，恐為所殺。

translations.literal: King personally requested Son of Heaven saying: ‘Body long in Han—now returning—weak alone—yet former king has son present—fear being killed by him.’
translations.idiomatic: Weituqi pleaded: ‘I grew up in Han—going home weak—the old king’s son may murder me.’

sentences.zh: 國中有伊循城，其地肥美，願漢遣一將屯田積穀，令臣得依其威重。

translations.literal: Within state is Yixun city—its land fertile beautiful—wish Han dispatch one general garrison-farm stockpile grain—order your servant obtain relying on its awe-weight.’
translations.idiomatic: ‘Plant a Han colonel at fertile Yixun so I may shelter under his spear.’

sentences.zh: 」於是漢遣司馬一人、吏士四十人，田伊循以填撫之。

translations.literal: ’ Thereupon Han dispatched Major one—clerks soldiers forty—farm Yixun to settle soothe them.
translations.idiomatic: ’ Han posted one major and forty men to farm Yixun as guarantors.

sentences.zh: 其後更置都尉。

translations.literal: Afterward again placed Commandant.
translations.idiomatic: Later a commandant replaced him.

sentences.zh: 伊循官置始此矣。

translations.literal: Yixun official placement began here.
translations.idiomatic: The Yixun post dates from this.

sentences.zh: 鄯善當漢道沖，西通且末七百二十里。

translations.literal: Shanshan sits Han road’s hub—west connects Qiemo 720 li.
translations.idiomatic: Shanshan straddled the highway—seven hundred twenty li west to Qiemo.

sentences.zh: 自且末以往皆種五穀，土地草木，畜產作兵，略與漢同，有異乃記雲。

translations.literal: From Qiemo onward all plant five grains—land soil grasses trees—livestock production weapon-making—roughly same as Han—where different then record it.
translations.idiomatic: Beyond Qiemo every oasis grew the five grains with Han-like farms, herds, and arms—only oddities merit note.

sentences.zh: 且末國，王治且末城，去長安六千八百二十里。

translations.literal: Qiemo state—king rules Qiemo city—6820 li from Chang’an.
translations.idiomatic: Qiemo—capital Qiemo—lies sixty-eight hundred twenty li from Chang’an.

sentences.zh: 戶二百三十，口千六百一十，勝兵三百二十人。

translations.literal: Households two hundred thirty, mouths one thousand six hundred ten, victors-in-arms three hundred twenty persons.
translations.idiomatic: It had 230 households, 1,610 people, and 320 men fit for military service.

sentences.zh: 輔國侯、左右將、譯長各一人。

translations.literal: State-supporting marquis, left and right generals, chief interpreter—each one person.
translations.idiomatic: Its officials included one state-supporting marquis, one general of the left and one of the right, and one chief interpreter.

sentences.zh: 西北至都護治所二千二百五十八裡，北接尉犁，南至小宛可三日行。

translations.literal: Northwest to the Protector-General's governing seat two thousand two hundred fifty-eight li; north adjoins Yuli; south to Xiaowan is a journey of three days.
translations.idiomatic: It lay 2,258 li northwest of the Protector-General's headquarters; Yuli bordered it on the north, and Xiaowan was three days' travel to the south.

sentences.zh: 有蒲陶諸果。

translations.literal: Has grapes and various fruits.
translations.idiomatic: Grapes and other fruits grew there.

sentences.zh: 西通精絕二千里。

translations.literal: West connects to Jingjue two thousand li.
translations.idiomatic: A route westward ran two thousand li to Jingjue.

sentences.zh: 小宛國，王治扜零城，去長安七千二百一十里。

translations.literal: The state of Xiaowan—the king rules Yu-ling city—seven thousand two hundred ten li from Chang'an.
translations.idiomatic: The kingdom of Xiaowan had its royal seat at Yu-ling, seven thousand two hundred ten li from Chang'an.

sentences.zh: 戶百五十，口千五十，勝兵二百人。

translations.literal: Households one hundred fifty, mouths one thousand fifty, victors-in-arms two hundred persons.
translations.idiomatic: It counted 150 households, 1,050 people, and 200 soldiers.

sentences.zh: 輔國侯、左右都尉各一人。

translations.literal: State-supporting marquis, left and right commandants—each one person.
translations.idiomatic: There were one state-supporting marquis and one commandant of the left and one of the right.

sentences.zh: 西北至都護治所二千五百五十八里，東與婼羌接，辟南不當道。

translations.literal: Northwest to the Protector-General's governing seat two thousand five hundred fifty-eight li; east adjoins Ruoqiang; south side remote, does not face the road.
translations.idiomatic: It was 2,558 li northwest of the Protector-General's seat; Ruoqiang lay to the east, and its southern flank stood aside from the main highway.

sentences.zh: 精絕國，王治精絕城，去長安八千八百二十里。

translations.literal: The state of Jingjue—the king rules Jingjue city—eight thousand eight hundred twenty li from Chang'an.
translations.idiomatic: The kingdom of Jingjue had its capital at Jingjue, eight thousand eight hundred twenty li from Chang'an.

sentences.zh: 戶四百八十，口三千三百六十，勝兵五百人。

translations.literal: Households four hundred eighty, mouths three thousand three hundred sixty, victors-in-arms five hundred persons.
translations.idiomatic: It had 480 households, 3,360 inhabitants, and 500 troops.

sentences.zh: 精絕都尉、左右將、譯長各一人。

translations.literal: Jingjue commandant, left and right generals, chief interpreter—each one person.
translations.idiomatic: Its administration included one Jingjue commandant, one general of the left and one of the right, and one chief interpreter.

sentences.zh: 北至都護治所二千七百二十三里南至戎盧國四日行，地厄狹，西通抒彌四百六十里。

translations.literal: North to the Protector-General's governing seat two thousand seven hundred twenty-three li; south to the state of Ronglu four days' journey; terrain perilous and narrow; west connects to Yumi four hundred sixty li.
translations.idiomatic: It stood 2,723 li north of the Protector-General's headquarters and four days' march south of Ronglu; the ground was rugged and tight, and a western route ran 460 li to Yumi.

sentences.zh: 戎盧國，王治卑品城，去長安八千三百里。

translations.literal: The state of Ronglu—the king rules Beipin city—eight thousand three hundred li from Chang'an.
translations.idiomatic: The state of Ronglu ruled from Beipin, eight thousand three hundred li from Chang'an.

sentences.zh: 戶二百四十，口千六百一十，勝兵三百人。

translations.literal: Households two hundred forty, mouths one thousand six hundred ten, victors-in-arms three hundred persons.
translations.idiomatic: It recorded 240 households, 1,610 people, and 300 soldiers.

sentences.zh: 東北至都護治所二千八百五十八里，東與小宛、南與婼羌、西與渠勒接，辟南不當道。

translations.literal: Northeast to the Protector-General's governing seat two thousand eight hundred fifty-eight li; east adjoins Xiaowan, south adjoins Ruoqiang, west adjoins Qule; south side remote, does not face the road.
translations.idiomatic: It lay 2,858 li northeast of the Protector-General's seat, bordering Xiaowan on the east, Ruoqiang on the south, and Qule on the west, with its southern approaches off the main road.

sentences.zh: 抒彌國，王治抒彌城，去長安九千二百八十里。

translations.literal: The state of Yumi—the king rules Yumi city—nine thousand two hundred eighty li from Chang'an.
translations.idiomatic: The kingdom of Yumi had its seat at Yumi, nine thousand two hundred eighty li from Chang'an.

sentences.zh: 戶三千三百四十，口二萬四十，勝兵三千五百四十人。

translations.literal: Households three thousand three hundred forty, mouths twenty thousand forty, victors-in-arms three thousand five hundred forty persons.
translations.idiomatic: It comprised 3,340 households, 20,040 people, and 3,540 fighting men.

sentences.zh: 輔國侯、左右將、左右都尉、左右騎君各一人，譯長二人。

translations.literal: State-supporting marquis, left and right generals, left and right commandants, left and right cavalry chiefs—each one person; chief interpreters two persons.
translations.idiomatic: Officials included one state-supporting marquis, one general and one commandant on each flank, one cavalry chief on each flank, and two chief interpreters.

sentences.zh: 東北至都護治所三千五百五十三里，南與渠勒、東北與龜茲、西北與姑墨接，西通于闐三百九十里。

translations.literal: Northeast to the Protector-General's governing seat three thousand five hundred fifty-three li; south adjoins Qule, northeast adjoins Qiuci, northwest adjoins Gumo; west connects to Yutian three hundred ninety li.
translations.idiomatic: It was 3,553 li northeast of the Protector-General's headquarters, bordered Qule on the south, Kucha on the northeast, and Gumo on the northwest, and linked westward with Yutian across 390 li.

sentences.zh: 今名寧彌。

translations.literal: Now named Ningmi.
translations.idiomatic: Today the place is known as Ningmi.

sentences.zh: 渠勒國，王治鞬都城，去長安九千九百五十里。

translations.literal: The state of Qule—the king rules Jiandu city—nine thousand nine hundred fifty li from Chang'an.
translations.idiomatic: The state of Qule ruled from Jiandu, nine thousand nine hundred fifty li from Chang'an.

sentences.zh: 戶三百一十，口二千一百七十，勝兵三百人。

translations.literal: Households three hundred ten, mouths two thousand one hundred seventy, victors-in-arms three hundred persons.
translations.idiomatic: It had 310 households, 2,170 people, and 300 troops.

sentences.zh: 東北至都護治所三千八百五十二里，東與戎盧、西與婼羌、北與抒彌接。

translations.literal: Northeast to the Protector-General's governing seat three thousand eight hundred fifty-two li; east adjoins Ronglu, west adjoins Ruoqiang, north adjoins Yumi.
translations.idiomatic: It lay 3,852 li northeast of the Protector-General's seat, with Ronglu on the east, Ruoqiang on the west, and Yumi on the north.

sentences.zh: 于闐國，王治西城，去長安九千六百七十里。

translations.literal: The state of Yutian—the king rules the western city—nine thousand six hundred seventy li from Chang'an.
translations.idiomatic: The kingdom of Yutian had its royal residence at the western capital, nine thousand six hundred seventy li from Chang'an.

sentences.zh: 戶三千三百，口萬九千三百，勝兵二千四百人。

translations.literal: Households three thousand three hundred, mouths nineteen thousand three hundred, victors-in-arms two thousand four hundred persons.
translations.idiomatic: It numbered 3,300 households, 19,300 people, and 2,400 soldiers.

sentences.zh: 輔國侯、左右將、左右騎君、東西城長、譯長各一人。

translations.literal: State-supporting marquis, left and right generals, left and right cavalry chiefs, chiefs of eastern and western cities, chief interpreter—each one person.
translations.idiomatic: There were one state-supporting marquis, one general and one cavalry chief on each flank, one magistrate each for the eastern and western cities, and one chief interpreter.

sentences.zh: 東北至都護治所三千九百四十七里，南與婼羌接，北與姑墨接。

translations.literal: Northeast to the Protector-General's governing seat three thousand nine hundred forty-seven li; south adjoins Ruoqiang, north adjoins Gumo.
translations.idiomatic: It stood 3,947 li northeast of the Protector-General's headquarters, adjoining Ruoqiang on the south and Gumo on the north.

sentences.zh: 于闐之西，水皆西流，注西海；

translations.literal: West of Yutian, waters all flow west, pour into the Western Sea;
translations.idiomatic: West of Yutian every stream ran westward toward the Western Sea;

sentences.zh: 其東，水東流，注鹽澤，河原出焉。

translations.literal: Its east, waters flow east, pour into the Salt Marsh; the river's source issues there.
translations.idiomatic: east of the kingdom the rivers flowed east into the Salt Marsh, where the Yellow River had its source.

sentences.zh: 多玉石。

translations.literal: Much jade and stone.
translations.idiomatic: The country produced abundant jade.

sentences.zh: 西通皮山三百八十里。

translations.literal: West connects to Pishan three hundred eighty li.
translations.idiomatic: A western road ran 380 li to Pishan.

sentences.zh: 皮山國，王治皮山城，去長安萬五十里。

translations.literal: The state of Pishan—the king rules Pishan city—ten thousand fifty li from Chang'an.
translations.idiomatic: The kingdom of Pishan was centered on Pishan, ten thousand fifty li from Chang'an.

sentences.zh: 戶五百，口三千五百，勝兵五百人。

translations.literal: Households five hundred, mouths three thousand five hundred, victors-in-arms five hundred persons.
translations.idiomatic: It had 500 households, 3,500 people, and 500 soldiers.

sentences.zh: 左右將、左右都尉、騎君、譯長各一人。

translations.literal: Left and right generals, left and right commandants, cavalry chief, chief interpreter—each one person.
translations.idiomatic: Its roster listed one general of the left and one of the right, one commandant on each flank, one cavalry chief, and one chief interpreter.

sentences.zh: 東北至都護治所四千二百九十二里，西南至烏秅國千三百四十里，南與天篤接，北至姑墨千四百五十里，西南當罽賓、烏弋山離道，西北通莎車三百八十里。

translations.literal: Northeast to the Protector-General's governing seat four thousand two hundred ninety-two li; southwest to the state of Wucha one thousand three hundred forty li; south adjoins Tianzhu; north to Gumo one thousand four hundred fifty li; southwest lies on the road to Jibin and Alexandria-under-the-Mountains; northwest connects to Shache three hundred eighty li.
translations.idiomatic: It lay 4,292 li northeast of the Protector-General's seat and 1,340 li southwest of Wucha; India bordered it on the south, Gumo stood 1,450 li to the north, the southwest lay on the corridor toward Jibin and Alexandria Prophthasia, and Shache was 380 li to the northwest.

sentences.zh: 烏秅國，王治烏秅城，去長安九千九百五十里。

translations.literal: The state of Wucha—the king rules Wucha city—nine thousand nine hundred fifty li from Chang'an.
translations.idiomatic: The polity of Wucha had its capital at Wucha, nine thousand nine hundred fifty li from Chang'an.

sentences.zh: 戶四百九十，口二千七百三十三，勝兵七百四十人。

translations.literal: Households four hundred ninety, mouths two thousand seven hundred thirty-three, victors-in-arms seven hundred forty persons.
translations.idiomatic: It counted 490 households, 2,733 inhabitants, and 740 warriors.

sentences.zh: 東北至都護治所四千八百九十二里，北與子合、蒲犁，西與難兜接。

translations.literal: Northeast to the Protector-General's governing seat four thousand eight hundred ninety-two li; north adjoins Zihe and Puli, west adjoins Nandou.
translations.idiomatic: It was 4,892 li northeast of the Protector-General's headquarters, bordered Zihe and Puli on the north, and touched Nandou on the west.

sentences.zh: 山居，田石間。

translations.literal: Mountain dwelling; till among stones.
translations.idiomatic: The people lived in the mountains and scratched fields from the rock.

sentences.zh: 有白草。

translations.literal: Has white grass.
translations.idiomatic: White grass grew there.

sentences.zh: 累石為室。

translations.literal: Pile stones to make chambers.
translations.idiomatic: They stacked stone into dwellings.

sentences.zh: 民接手飲。

translations.literal: The people join hands to drink.
translations.idiomatic: Villagers passed water hand to hand to drink.

sentences.zh: 出小步馬，有驢無牛。

translations.literal: Produces small pace-horses; has donkeys, no oxen.
translations.idiomatic: The land bred small pace horses and donkeys but no cattle.

sentences.zh: 其西則有縣度，去陽關五千八百八十八里，去都護治所五千二十里。

translations.literal: Its west then has the Suspended Crossing; from Yang Pass five thousand eight hundred eighty-eight li; from the Protector-General's governing seat five thousand twenty li.
translations.idiomatic: To the west lay the Suspended Crossing, 5,888 li from Yang Pass and 5,020 li from the Protector-General's seat.

sentences.zh: 縣度者，石山也，溪谷不通，以繩索相引而度雲。

translations.literal: The Suspended Crossing is stone mountains; ravine valleys do not connect; by ropes and cords mutually pulling, they cross—it is said.
translations.idiomatic: The Crossing was a wilderness of stone peaks where gorges did not link up; travelers reportedly hauled one another across on ropes.

sentences.zh: 西夜國，王號子合王，治呼犍谷，去長安萬二百五十里。

translations.literal: The state of Xiye—the king's title is King of Zihe—rules Hujian valley—ten thousand two hundred fifty li from Chang'an.
translations.idiomatic: The kingdom of Xiye was ruled by the king styled lord of Zihe from Hujian Valley, ten thousand two hundred fifty li from Chang'an.

sentences.zh: 戶三百五十，口四千，勝兵千人。

translations.literal: Households three hundred fifty, mouths four thousand, victors-in-arms one thousand persons.
translations.idiomatic: It comprised 350 households, 4,000 people, and 1,000 soldiers.

sentences.zh: 東北到都護治所五千四十六里，東與皮山、西南與烏秅、北與莎車、西與蒲犁接。

translations.literal: Northeast to the Protector-General's governing seat five thousand forty-six li; east adjoins Pishan, southwest adjoins Wucha, north adjoins Shache, west adjoins Puli.
translations.idiomatic: It stood 5,046 li northeast of the Protector-General's headquarters, adjoining Pishan on the east, Wucha on the southwest, Shache on the north, and Puli on the west.

sentences.zh: 蒲犁及依耐、無雷國皆西夜類也。

translations.literal: Puli and Yinai and Wulei states are all of Xiye category.
translations.idiomatic: Puli together with Yinai and Wulei belonged to the same cultural sphere as Xiye.

sentences.zh: 西夜與胡異，其種類羌氐行國，隨畜逐水草往來。

translations.literal: Xiye differs from the Hu; its kinds are Qiang-Di mobile states, following livestock pursuing water and grass back and forth.
translations.idiomatic: Unlike the Hu peoples, the Xiye cluster were Qiang and Di pastoralists who moved with their herds between seasonal pastures.

sentences.zh: 而子合土地出玉石。

translations.literal: And Zihe's territory produces jade and stone.
translations.idiomatic: Zihe's lands yielded jade.

sentences.zh: 蒲犁國，王治蒲犁谷，去長安九千五百五十里。

translations.literal: The state of Puli—the king rules Puli valley—nine thousand five hundred fifty li from Chang'an.
translations.idiomatic: The state of Puli ruled from Puli Valley, nine thousand five hundred fifty li from Chang'an.

sentences.zh: 戶六百五十，口五千，勝兵二千人。

translations.literal: Households six hundred fifty, mouths five thousand, victors-in-arms two thousand persons.
translations.idiomatic: It mustered 650 households, 5,000 people, and 2,000 troops.

sentences.zh: 東北至都護治所五千三百九十六里，東至莎車五百四十里，北至疏勒五百五十里，南與西夜子合接，西至無雷五百四十里。

translations.literal: Northeast to the Protector-General's governing seat five thousand three hundred ninety-six li; east to Shache five hundred forty li; north to Shule five hundred fifty li; south adjoins Xiye-Zihe; west to Wulei five hundred forty li.
translations.idiomatic: It lay 5,396 li northeast of the Protector-General's seat—540 li west of Shache, 550 li south of Shule, bordering Xiye and Zihe on the south, and 540 li east of Wulei.

sentences.zh: 侯、都尉各一人。

translations.literal: Marquis, commandant—each one person.
translations.idiomatic: There was one marquis and one commandant.

sentences.zh: 寄田莎車。

translations.literal: Lodged-field cultivation in Shache.
translations.idiomatic: Its farmers leased fields in Shache.

sentences.zh: 種俗與子合同。

translations.literal: Race and customs same as Zihe.
translations.idiomatic: Ethnic composition and customs matched those of Zihe.

sentences.zh: 依耐國，王治去長安萬一百五十里。

translations.literal: The state of Yinai—the king's governing seat—ten thousand one hundred fifty li from Chang'an.
translations.idiomatic: The kingdom of Yinai had its royal seat ten thousand one hundred fifty li from Chang'an.

sentences.zh: 戶一百二十五，口六百七十，勝兵三百五十人。

translations.literal: Households one hundred twenty-five, mouths six hundred seventy, victors-in-arms three hundred fifty persons.
translations.idiomatic: It recorded 125 households, 670 people, and 350 soldiers.

sentences.zh: 東北至都護治所二千七百三十里，至莎車五百四十里，至無雷五百四十里，北至疏勒六百五十里，南與子合接，俗相與同。

translations.literal: Northeast to the Protector-General's governing seat two thousand seven hundred thirty li; to Shache five hundred forty li; to Wulei five hundred forty li; north to Shule six hundred fifty li; south adjoins Zihe; customs mutually same.
translations.idiomatic: It was 2,730 li northeast of the Protector-General's headquarters, 540 li from both Shache and Wulei, 650 li south of Shule, bordered Zihe on the south, and shared Zihe's way of life.

sentences.zh: 少谷，寄田疏勒、莎車。

translations.literal: Few grains; lodged-field cultivation in Shule and Shache.
translations.idiomatic: Grain was scarce, so people rented farmland in Shule and Shache.

sentences.zh: 無雷國，王治無雷城，去長安九千九百五十里。

translations.literal: The state of Wulei—the king rules Wulei city—nine thousand nine hundred fifty li from Chang'an.
translations.idiomatic: The state of Wulei ruled from Wulei, nine thousand nine hundred fifty li from Chang'an.

sentences.zh: 戶千，口七千，勝兵三千人。

translations.literal: Households one thousand, mouths seven thousand, victors-in-arms three thousand persons.
translations.idiomatic: It had 1,000 households, 7,000 people, and 3,000 troops.

sentences.zh: 東北至都護治所二千四百六十五里，南至蒲犁五百四十里，南與烏秅、北與捐毒、西與大月氏接。

translations.literal: Northeast to the Protector-General's governing seat two thousand four hundred sixty-five li; south to Puli five hundred forty li; south adjoins Wucha, north adjoins Juandu, west adjoins the Great Yuezhi.
translations.idiomatic: It lay 2,465 li northeast of the Protector-General's seat and 540 li north of Puli, bordering Wucha on the south, Juandu on the north, and the Great Yuezhi on the west.

sentences.zh: 衣服類烏孫，俗與子合同。

translations.literal: Clothing resembles Wusun; customs same as Zihe.
translations.idiomatic: Dress resembled that of Wusun, while customs followed Zihe's pattern.

sentences.zh: 難兜國，王治去長安萬一百五十里。

translations.literal: The state of Nandou—the king's governing seat—ten thousand one hundred fifty li from Chang'an.
translations.idiomatic: The kingdom of Nandou had its court ten thousand one hundred fifty li from Chang'an.

sentences.zh: 戶五千，口三萬一千，勝兵八千人。

translations.literal: Households five thousand, mouths thirty-one thousand, victors-in-arms eight thousand persons.
translations.idiomatic: It comprised 5,000 households, 31,000 inhabitants, and 8,000 warriors.

sentences.zh: 東北至都護治所二千八百五十里，南至無雷三百四十里，西南至罽賓三百三十里，南與婼羌、北與休循、西與大月氏接。

translations.literal: Northeast to the Protector-General's governing seat two thousand eight hundred fifty li; south to Wulei three hundred forty li; southwest to Jibin three hundred thirty li; south adjoins Ruoqiang, north adjoins Xiuxun, west adjoins the Great Yuezhi.
translations.idiomatic: It stood 2,850 li northeast of the Protector-General's headquarters, 340 li north of Wulei, and 330 li northeast of Jibin, with Ruoqiang on the south, Xiuxun on the north, and the Great Yuezhi on the west.

sentences.zh: 種五穀、蒲陶諸果。

translations.literal: Plants the five grains, grapes, and various fruits.
translations.idiomatic: Farmers grew the staple cereals, grapes, and other fruit.

sentences.zh: 有金、銀、銅、鐵，作兵與諸國同，屬罽賓。

translations.literal: Has gold, silver, copper, iron; makes weapons like the various states; belongs to Jibin.
translations.idiomatic: Gold, silver, copper, and iron were mined, weapons matched neighboring styles, and the realm was tributary to Jibin.

sentences.zh: 罽賓國，王治循鮮城，去長安萬二千二百里。

translations.literal: The state of Jibin—the king rules Xunxian city—twelve thousand two hundred li from Chang'an.
translations.idiomatic: The kingdom of Jibin ruled from Xunxian, twelve thousand two hundred li from Chang'an.

sentences.zh: 不屬都護。

translations.literal: Does not belong to the Protector-General.
translations.idiomatic: It lay outside the Protector-General's jurisdiction.

sentences.zh: 戶口勝兵多，大國也。

translations.literal: Households, mouths, victors-in-arms many—it is a great state.
translations.idiomatic: Its households, population, and army were large; it ranked as a major power.

sentences.zh: 東北至都護治所六千八百四十里，東至烏秅國二千二百五十里，東北至難兜國九日行，西北與大月氏、西南與烏弋山離接。

translations.literal: Northeast to the Protector-General's governing seat six thousand eight hundred forty li; east to the state of Wucha two thousand two hundred fifty li; northeast to the state of Nandou nine days' journey; northwest adjoins the Great Yuezhi, southwest adjoins Alexandria-under-the-Mountains.
translations.idiomatic: It was 6,840 li northeast of the Protector-General's seat, 2,250 li west of Wucha, and nine days' march southwest of Nandou; the Great Yuezhi bordered it on the northwest and Alexandria Prophthasia on the southwest.

sentences.zh: 昔匈奴破大月氏，大月氏西君大夏，而塞王南君罽賓。

translations.literal: Formerly the Xiongnu destroyed the Great Yuezhi; the Great Yuezhi westward ruled Great Xia as lord, while the Sai king southward ruled Jibin as lord.
translations.idiomatic: Long ago the Xiongnu shattered the Great Yuezhi; the Yuezhi then moved west to rule Daxia, while the Sai king pressed south to dominate Jibin.

sentences.zh: 塞種分散，往往為數國。

translations.literal: The Sai stock scattered; often became several states.
translations.idiomatic: The Sai peoples broke apart and repeatedly founded separate realms.

sentences.zh: 自疏勒以西北，休循、捐毒之屬，皆故塞種也。

translations.literal: From Shule toward the northwest, Xiuxun, Juandu, and their kinds—all are former Sai stock.
translations.idiomatic: Northwest of Shule, Xiuxun, Juandu, and related states were all descendants of the Sai.

sentences.zh: 罽賓地平，溫和，有目宿、雜草、奇木、檀、榱、梓、竹、漆。

translations.literal: Jibin's land is level, temperate and mild; has alfalfa, mixed grasses, strange trees, sandalwood, nanmu, catalpa, bamboo, lacquer.
translations.idiomatic: Jibin's terrain was flat and mild, rich in alfalfa, mixed herbage, exotic timber, sandalwood, hardwoods, catalpa, bamboo, and lacquer trees.

sentences.zh: 種五穀、蒲陶諸果，糞治園田。

translations.literal: Plants the five grains, grapes, and various fruits; manures and tends gardens and fields.
translations.idiomatic: They cultivated cereals, grapes, and orchard crops and fertilized their gardens intensively.

sentences.zh: 地下濕，生稻，冬食生菜。

translations.literal: Ground low and damp; grows rice; winter eats raw vegetables.
translations.idiomatic: Low, humid soil supported rice, and even in winter people ate fresh greens.

sentences.zh: 其民巧，雕文刻鏤，治宮室，織罽，刺文繡，好酒食。

translations.literal: Its people are skilled; carve patterns and inlay; build palaces; weave felts; embroider patterned silks; fond of wine and food.
translations.idiomatic: The inhabitants were artisans who carved and inlaid ornament, raised fine houses, wove wool tapestry, embroidered silk, and delighted in feasting.

sentences.zh: 有金、銀、銅、錫，以為器。

translations.literal: Has gold, silver, copper, tin; makes them into vessels.
translations.idiomatic: Gold, silver, copper, and tin were worked into household goods.

sentences.zh: 市列。

translations.literal: Market rows.
translations.idiomatic: Market stalls lined the streets.

sentences.zh: 以金銀為錢，文為騎馬，幕為人面。

translations.literal: Uses gold and silver for coin; pattern is mounted horse; reverse is human face.
translations.idiomatic: They minted gold and silver coins stamped with a horseman on the obverse and a human face on the reverse.

sentences.zh: 出封牛、水牛、象、大狗、沐猴、孔爵、珠璣、珊瑚、虜魄、璧流離。

translations.literal: Produces humped cattle, water buffalo, elephant, large dog, macaque, peacock, pearls, coral, amber, colored glass.
translations.idiomatic: Exports included zebu, water buffalo, elephants, mastiffs, macaques, peacocks, pearls, coral, amber, and faience beads.

sentences.zh: 它畜與諸國同。

translations.literal: Other livestock same as the various states.
translations.idiomatic: Other livestock resembled those of neighboring countries.

sentences.zh: 自武帝始通罽賓，自以絕遠，漢兵不能至，其王烏頭勞數剽殺漢使。

translations.literal: From Emperor Wu they first opened communication with Jibin; [Jibin] deemed itself utterly remote—Han troops could not arrive—its king Wutoulao repeatedly raided and killed Han envoys.
translations.idiomatic: Contacts began under Emperor Wu, yet Jibin considered itself beyond Han reach; King Wutoulao repeatedly ambushed and murdered imperial envoys.

sentences.zh: 烏頭勞死，子代立，遣使奉獻。

translations.literal: Wutoulao died; his son succeeded and stood; sent envoys bearing tribute.
translations.idiomatic: When Wutoulao died his son succeeded and dispatched a mission with tribute.

sentences.zh: 漢使關都尉文忠送其使。

translations.literal: Han envoy Guanduwei Wen Zhong escorted their envoys.
translations.idiomatic: The Han court assigned Colonel of the Pass Wen Zhong to escort the returning embassy.

sentences.zh: 王復欲害忠，忠覺之，乃與容屈王子陰末赴共合謀，攻罽賓，殺其王，立陰末赴為罽賓王，授印綬。

translations.literal: The king again wished to harm Zhong; Zhong perceived it; then with Rongqu prince Yinmofu together jointly plotted, attacked Jibin, killed its king, established Yinmofu as king of Jibin, invested seal and ribbon.
translations.idiomatic: The king plotted again to murder Zhong, but Zhong learned of it and conspired with Yinmofu, prince of Rongqu; they struck Jibin, slew the monarch, enthroned Yinmofu, and Han invested him with seal and cords.

sentences.zh: 後軍候趙德使罽賓，與陰末赴相失，陰末赴鎖琅當德，殺副已下七十餘人，遣使者上書謝。

translations.literal: Later army scout Zhao De was envoy to Jibin; with Yinmofu mutually fell out; Yinmofu shackled fetters bound De; killed deputy and below seventy-odd persons; sent envoys with memorial apologizing.
translations.idiomatic: Later the army guide Zhao De visited Jibin and quarreled with Yinmofu, who chained him, slaughtered more than seventy men down to the deputy envoy, then sent a letter of apology.

sentences.zh: 孝元帝以絕域不錄，放其使者於縣度，絕而不通。

translations.literal: Emperor Xiaoyuan, because it was a cut-off region, did not register [punishment]; released their envoys at the Suspended Crossing; cut off and did not communicate.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Yuan dismissed the affair because the realm seemed unreachable, turned the envoys back at the Suspended Crossing, and severed relations.

sentences.zh: 成帝時，復遣使獻謝罪，漢欲遣使者報送其使，杜欽說大將軍王鳳曰：「前罽賓王陰末赴本漢所立，後卒畔逆。

translations.literal: Under Emperor Cheng they again sent envoys offering tribute and apologizing; Han wished to dispatch envoys replying and escorting their envoys; Du Qin exhorted Grand General Wang Feng, saying: "Formerly the Jibin king Yinmofu was originally Han-established; afterward finally rebelled.
translations.idiomatic: During Emperor Cheng's reign Jibin again sent envoys with gifts and contrition. When Han proposed escorting them home, Du Qin urged Grand General Wang Feng: "The last king of Jibin, Yinmofu, owed his throne to Han, yet he ultimately turned traitor.

sentences.zh: 夫德莫大於有國子民，罪莫大於執殺使者，所以不報恩，不懼誅者，自知絕遠，兵不至也。

translations.literal: Now virtue none greater than possessing a state and nurturing the people; crime none greater than seizing and killing envoys; the reason they do not repay kindness and do not fear execution—self-know utterly remote, troops will not arrive.
translations.idiomatic: No virtue surpasses ruling a state and caring for its people; no crime surpasses murdering ambassadors. They feel no gratitude and no fear of punishment because they know themselves impossibly distant and beyond Han's armies.

sentences.zh: 有求則卑辭，無慾則嬌嫚，終不可懷服。

translations.literal: When they have requests then humble speech; when without desires then arrogant contempt; in the end cannot be cherished into submission.
translations.idiomatic: They crawl when they want something and sneer when they do not; they will never be won by kindness.

sentences.zh: 凡中國所以通厚蠻夷，愜快其求者，為壤比而為寇也。

translations.literal: In general the Central Lands' reason for communicating generously with barbarians and satisfying their requests is because their lands border and become raiders.
translations.idiomatic: The Central States indulge frontier peoples chiefly when those peoples neighbor Han territory and might raid if refused.

sentences.zh: 今縣度之厄，非罽賓所能越也。

translations.literal: Now the Suspended Crossing's peril—not something Jibin can cross.
translations.idiomatic: The lethal terrain of the Suspended Crossing lies wholly beyond Jibin's ability to cross toward us.

sentences.zh: 其鄉慕，不足以安西域，雖不附，不能危城郭。

translations.literal: Their inclination to admire is insufficient to pacify the Western Regions; although not submitting, cannot endanger cities and walls.
translations.idiomatic: Their professions of admiration cannot stabilize the Western Regions, yet withholding tribute cannot threaten our walled cities.

sentences.zh: 前親逆節，惡暴西城，故絕而不通；

translations.literal: Earlier personally violated duty; wicked violence in the western cities; therefore cut off and did not communicate;
translations.idiomatic: They once betrayed their duty and terrorized the western frontier, so relations were severed;

sentences.zh: 今悔過來，而無親屬貴人，奉獻者皆行賈賤人，欲通貨市買，以獻為名，故煩使者送至縣度，恐失實見欺。

translations.literal: Now repenting they come, yet without kinsmen or noble persons; those presenting tribute are all traveling merchant low persons; wish to circulate goods and market-buy; take tribute as name; therefore troubling envoys send to the Suspended Crossing; fear losing truth and being deceived.
translations.idiomatic: Now they profess repentance, yet no nobles or royal kin accompany them; everyone bearing gifts is a petty pedlar seeking trade under cover of tribute. Having envoys escort them clear to the Suspended Crossing courts deception and misjudgment.

sentences.zh: 凡遣使送客者，欲為防護寇害也。

translations.literal: In general dispatching envoys escorting guests—wish to act as guard against bandit harm.
translations.idiomatic: Envoys escort foreign parties precisely to shield them from robbery and attack.

sentences.zh: 起皮山南，更不屬漢之國四五，斥候士百餘人，五分夜擊刀斗自守，尚時為所侵盜。

translations.literal: Starting south of Pishan, further not belonging to Han—states four or five; sentry soldiers over one hundred persons; divide night fifths strike knife-guards defend themselves; still sometimes by them invaded and plundered.
translations.idiomatic: South of Pishan lie four or five polities no longer under Han. Even when more than a hundred scouts split the night watches and strike signal-clappers to hold their ground, raiders still strike.

sentences.zh: 驢畜負糧，須諸國稟食，得以自贍。

translations.literal: Donkeys and livestock bear grain; must from the various states receive food; thereby able self-sustain.
translations.idiomatic: Pack donkeys haul grain, yet columns must beg provisions from each state along the way or starve.

sentences.zh: 國或貧小不能食，或桀黠不肯給，擁強漢之節，餒山谷之間，乞匄無所得，離一二旬則人畜棄捐曠野而不反。

translations.literal: States perhaps poor small cannot feed; or stubborn crafty unwilling supply; clutch strong Han's credentials; starve among mountain valleys; beg nothing obtained; separate one-two ten-day periods then men livestock abandoned cast wilderness and not return.
translations.idiomatic: Some realms are too poor to feed them; others defy Han credentials and refuse grain. Envoys clutch imperial batons yet waste away in the hills, begging in vain; within a fortnight men and beasts lie rotting on the desert and never come home.

sentences.zh: 又歷大頭痛、小頭痛之山，赤土、身熱之阪，令人身熱無色，頭痛嘔吐，驢畜盡然。

translations.literal: Further pass Great Headache, Little Headache mountains; Red Earth, Body Fever slopes; cause men body fever without color, headache vomit; donkeys livestock all likewise.
translations.idiomatic: Beyond lie the Great and Little Headache ranges and the Red-Earth and Fever slopes, where travelers burn with fever, turn ash-gray, and retch with splitting headaches; beasts suffer the same.

sentences.zh: 又有三池、盤石阪，道狹者尺六七寸，長者徑三十里。

translations.literal: Further have Three Pools, Coiled Stone slopes; road narrow places one foot six-seven inches; long places extent thirty li.
translations.idiomatic: There are also the Three Pools and the Coiled-Stone defiles, where the track shrinks to sixteen or seventeen inches yet stretches thirty li without widening.

sentences.zh: 臨崢嶸不測之深，行者騎步相持，繩索相引，二千餘裡乃到縣度。

translations.literal: Overlooking precipitous unfathomable depths; travelers horse foot mutually support; ropes cords mutually pull; two thousand-odd li then arrive Suspended Crossing.
translations.idiomatic: Sheer cliffs drop into depths no one can measure; riders and walkers brace one another and rope themselves together for more than two thousand li before they reach the Crossing.

sentences.zh: 畜隊，未半坑谷盡靡碎；

translations.literal: Livestock fall; not halfway pits valleys all pulverized shattered;
translations.idiomatic: When animals slip, long before the midpoint the gorges are strewn with shattered carcasses;

sentences.zh: 人墮，勢不得相收視。

translations.literal: Men fall; circumstance cannot mutually rescue look-after.
translations.idiomatic: when men tumble, no one can halt to recover the fallen.

sentences.zh: 險阻危害，不可勝言。

translations.literal: Peril obstacles harm danger; cannot exhaust speech.
translations.idiomatic: The dangers along that road beggar description.

sentences.zh: 聖王分九州，制五服，務盛內，不求外。

translations.literal: Sage kings divided nine provinces; regulated five domains; strove flourish interior; did not seek exterior.
translations.idiomatic: The sage kings carved out the Nine Provinces, drew the Five Domains, labored to enrich the interior, and did not chase gains beyond the frontier.

sentences.zh: 今遣使者承至尊之命，送蠻夷之賈，勞吏士之眾，涉危難之路，罷弊所恃以事無用，非久長計也。

translations.literal: Now dispatch envoys bearing Supreme one's mandate; escort barbarians' merchants; toil clerks soldiers' multitudes; ford peril hardship roads; exhaust weaken what is relied on to serve useless—not long-lasting plan.
translations.idiomatic: Today we would waste imperial envoys escorting barbarian traders, tire hosts of officers over lethal passes, and squander the very strengths we depend on for nothing lasting.

sentences.zh: 使者業已受節，可至皮山而還。

translations.literal: Envoy already has received credentials; may reach Pishan and return.
translations.idiomatic: The envoy already holds his credentials; let him escort them only as far as Pishan and turn back.

sentences.zh: 」於是鳳白從欽言。

translations.literal: Closed-quote thereupon Feng memorialized and followed Qin's words.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Feng reported Du Qin's opinion to the throne and adopted it.

sentences.zh: 罽賓實利賞賜賈市，其使數年而一至雲。

translations.literal: Jibin in truth profits gifts rewards market trade; its envoys several years then one arrival—it is said.
translations.idiomatic: Jibin cares chiefly for Han stipends and market profit; envoys show up only once every few years, or so it is said.

sentences.zh: 烏弋山離國，王去長安萬二千二百里。

translations.literal: Alexandria-under-the-Mountains state—king from Chang'an twelve thousand two hundred li.
translations.idiomatic: The kingdom of Alexandria Prophthasia lay twelve thousand two hundred li from Chang'an.

sentences.zh: 不屬都護。

translations.literal: Does not belong to Protector-General.
translations.idiomatic: It stood outside the Protector-General's jurisdiction.

sentences.zh: 戶口勝兵，大國也。

translations.literal: Households mouths victors-in-arms—a great state.
translations.idiomatic: Its households, population, and army were large—a major power.

sentences.zh: 東北至都護治所六十日行，東與罽賓、北與撲挑、西與犁靬、條支接。

translations.literal: Northeast to Protector-General governing seat sixty days' journey; east adjoins Jibin, north adjoins Pudiao, west adjoins Liqian, Tiaozhi.
translations.idiomatic: Sixty days' march northeast of the Protector-General's seat, it bordered Jibin on the east, Puda on the north, and Roman Syria and Tiaozhi on the west.

sentences.zh: 行可百餘日，乃至條支。

translations.literal: Journey about hundred-odd days, then reach Tiaozhi.
translations.idiomatic: Some hundred days of travel bring one to Tiaozhi.

sentences.zh: 國臨西海，暑濕，田稻。

translations.literal: State borders western sea; hot damp; fields rice.
translations.idiomatic: The realm fronts the western sea; heat and humidity breed wet-field rice.

sentences.zh: 有大鳥，卵如甕。

translations.literal: Has great birds; eggs like jars.
translations.idiomatic: Giant birds nest there with eggs as large as urns.

sentences.zh: 人眾甚多，往往有小君長，安息役屬之，以為外國。

translations.literal: People multitudes very many; often have petty lord chiefs; Anxi subjugates them; treats as outer state.
translations.idiomatic: The population is dense and petty chiefs abound; Parthia holds them in tributary service as an outer dependency.

sentences.zh: 善眩。

translations.literal: Skilled at dazzling.
translations.idiomatic: Its people excel at conjury and illusion.

sentences.zh: 安息長老傳聞條支有弱水、西王母，亦未嘗見也。

translations.literal: Anxi elders transmit-hear Tiaozhi has Weak Water, Queen Mother of the West; also never tasted see.
translations.idiomatic: Parthian elders repeat rumors that Tiaozhi holds the Weak River and the Queen Mother of the West, yet none has seen them.

sentences.zh: 自條支乘水西行，可百餘日，近日所入雲。

translations.literal: From Tiaozhi ride water west go; about hundred-odd days; approach sun's entering—it is said.
translations.idiomatic: From Tiaozhi one can sail westward another hundred days toward where the sun is said to set.

sentences.zh: 烏戈地暑熱莽平，其草木、畜產、五穀、果菜、食飲、宮室、市列、錢貨、兵器、金珠之屬皆與罽賓同，而有桃拔、師子、犀子。

translations.literal: Wuge land hot muggy level waste; its grasses trees, livestock produce, five grains, fruits vegetables, food drink, palaces houses, market rows, coin goods, weapons arms, gold pearls kinds—all same as Jibin; yet has taoba, lions, rhinos.
translations.idiomatic: Wugo's basin is torrid and flat; flora, fauna, grains, produce, diet, architecture, markets, coinage, arms, and gems match Jibin's, but it also yields taoba beasts, lions, and rhinoceroses.

sentences.zh: 俗重妄殺。

translations.literal: Custom heavily punishes wanton killing.
translations.idiomatic: Their laws treat arbitrary killing with exceptional severity.

sentences.zh: 其錢獨文為人頭，幕為騎馬。

translations.literal: Its coins alone pattern is human head; reverse is mounted horse.
translations.idiomatic: Their silver coins alone show a human head while the reverse bears a horseman.

sentences.zh: 以金銀飾杖。

translations.literal: Use gold silver decorate staves.
translations.idiomatic: Official staves are inlaid with gold and silver.

sentences.zh: 絕遠，漢使希至。

translations.literal: Utterly remote; Han envoys rarely arrive.
translations.idiomatic: So remote that Han embassies seldom reach it.

sentences.zh: 自玉門、陽關出南道，歷鄯善而南行，至烏弋山離，南道極矣。

translations.literal: From Yumen, Yang Pass exit southern route; pass Shanshan then south go; reach Alexandria-under-the-Mountains; southern route extreme.
translations.idiomatic: From Yumen and Yang Pass the southern road runs through Shanshan southward to Alexandria Prophthasia—the southern route's terminus.

sentences.zh: 轉北而東得安息。

translations.literal: Turn north then east obtain Anxi.
translations.idiomatic: Turn north and then eastward to reach Parthia.

sentences.zh: 安息國，王治番兜城，去長安萬一千六百里。

translations.literal: Anxi state—king rules Fandou city—eleven thousand six hundred li from Chang'an.
translations.idiomatic: Parthia ruled from Fandou, eleven thousand six hundred li from Chang'an.

sentences.zh: 不屬都護。

translations.literal: Does not belong to Protector-General.
translations.idiomatic: It lay beyond the Protector-General's authority.

sentences.zh: 北與康居、東與烏弋山離、西與條支接。

translations.literal: North adjoins Kangju, east adjoins Alexandria-under-the-Mountains, west adjoins Tiaozhi.
translations.idiomatic: Kangju bordered it on the north, Alexandria Prophthasia on the east, and Tiaozhi on the west.

sentences.zh: 土地風氣，物類所有，民俗與烏弋、罽賓同。

translations.literal: Land soil wind qi; things kinds possessed; folk customs with Wuge, Jibin same.
translations.idiomatic: Terrain, climate, products, and customs resemble those of Wugo and Jibin.

sentences.zh: 亦以銀為錢，文獨為王面，幕為夫人面。

translations.literal: Also uses silver for coin; pattern alone is king's face; reverse is madam's face.
translations.idiomatic: Silver coinage shows the king's portrait alone on the face and the queen on the reverse.

sentences.zh: 王死輒更鑄錢。

translations.literal: King dies then always recast coin.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever a king dies the realm remints its money.

sentences.zh: 有大馬爵。

translations.literal: Has great horse-sparrow.
translations.idiomatic: Great ostriches stride the plain.

sentences.zh: 其屬小大數百城，地方數千裡，最大國也。

translations.literal: Its dependencies small large several hundred cities; territory several thousand li—most great state.
translations.idiomatic: Its dependency towns number in the hundreds across thousands of li—the greatest realm of the west.

sentences.zh: 臨媯水，商賈車船行旁國。

translations.literal: Borders Gui water; merchants carts boats travel neighboring states.
translations.idiomatic: It straddles the Oxus, where merchants sail and caravan to surrounding kingdoms.

sentences.zh: 書草，旁行為書記。

translations.literal: Writes with brush drafting; sideways proceeding acts as documents.
translations.idiomatic: They draft records in a flowing hand and run lines sideways across the sheet.

sentences.zh: 武帝始遣使至安息，王令將將二萬騎迎於東界。

translations.literal: Emperor Wu first dispatched envoys reach Anxi; king ordered general lead twenty thousand horse greet at eastern border.
translations.idiomatic: When Emperor Wu first sent envoys to Parthia, the king ordered a commander to meet them at the eastern frontier with twenty thousand horsemen.

sentences.zh: 東界去王都數千里，行比至，過數十城，人民相屬。

translations.literal: Eastern border from royal capital several thousand li; journey when arrived, pass several tens cities; people mutually continuous.
translations.idiomatic: That border lay thousands of li from the capital; the ride in passed dozens of cities with continuous settlement.

sentences.zh: 因發使隨漢使者來觀漢地，以大鳥卵及犁靬眩人獻於漢，天子大悅。

translations.literal: Thereupon sent envoys follow Han envoys come view Han lands; with great bird eggs and Liqian dazzle-person offer to Han; Son of Heaven greatly pleased.
translations.idiomatic: Parthia then sent escorts back with the Han mission to tour the interior, presenting giant ostrich eggs and Syrian illusionists; the emperor was delighted.

sentences.zh: 安息東則大月氏。

translations.literal: Anxi east then Great Yuezhi.
translations.idiomatic: East of Parthia lies the Great Yuezhi.

sentences.zh: 大月氏國，治監氏城，去長安萬一千六百里。

translations.literal: Great Yuezhi state—rules Jianshi city—eleven thousand six hundred li from Chang'an.
translations.idiomatic: The Great Yuezhi ruled from Jianshi, eleven thousand six hundred li from Chang'an.

sentences.zh: 不屬都護。

translations.literal: Does not belong to Protector-General.
translations.idiomatic: It stood outside Han's Protector-General system.

sentences.zh: 戶十萬，口四十萬，勝兵十萬人。

translations.literal: Households one hundred thousand, mouths four hundred thousand, victors-in-arms one hundred thousand persons.
translations.idiomatic: It fielded 100,000 households, 400,000 people, and 100,000 soldiers.

sentences.zh: 東至都護治所四千七百四十里，西至安息四十九日行，南與罽賓接。

translations.literal: East to Protector-General governing seat four thousand seven hundred forty li; west to Anxi forty-nine days' journey; south adjoins Jibin.
translations.idiomatic: It lay 4,740 li west of the Protector-General's seat, forty-nine days from Parthia to the west, and bordered Jibin on the south.

sentences.zh: 土地風氣，物類所有，民俗錢貨，與安息同。

translations.literal: Land soil wind qi; things kinds possessed; folk customs coin goods—with Anxi same.
translations.idiomatic: Soil, climate, produce, customs, and currency resemble Parthia's.

sentences.zh: 出一封橐駝。

translations.literal: Produces one-hump camel.
translations.idiomatic: It exports single-humped Bactrian camels.

sentences.zh: 大月氏本行國也，隨畜移徙，與匈奴同俗。

translations.literal: Great Yuezhi originally mobile state; follow livestock move migrate—with Xiongnu same customs.
translations.idiomatic: The Great Yuezhi began as nomads who followed their herds like the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 控弦十餘萬，故強輕匈奴。

translations.literal: Bow-drawers over hundred thousand; therefore strong despised Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: More than a hundred thousand archers made them powerful enough to look down on the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 本居敦煌、祁連間，至昌頓單于攻破月氏，而老上單于殺月氏，以其頭為飲器，月氏乃遠去，過大宛，西擊大夏而臣之，都媯水北為王庭。

translations.literal: Originally dwelt between Dunhuang, Qilian; reached Modun Chanyu broke defeated Yuezhi; while Laoshang Chanyu killed Yuezhi, took his head as drinking vessel; Yuezhi then far departed, passed Dayuan, west struck Daxia and made them subjects, capital Gui water north as royal court.
translations.idiomatic: They once ranged between Dunhuang and the Qilian until Modun Chanyu shattered them and Laoshang Chanyu slew their king and fashioned his skull into a cup. The Yuezhi then fled far west beyond Dayuan, conquered Daxia, and built a royal camp north of the Oxus.

sentences.zh: 其餘小眾不能去者，保南山羌，號小月氏。

translations.literal: Those remainder small multitudes unable depart; shelter southern mountain Qiang; called Lesser Yuezhi.
translations.idiomatic: Smaller bands unable to flee sheltered among the Southern Mountain Qiang and were called the Lesser Yuezhi.

sentences.zh: 大夏本無大君長，城邑往往置小長，民弱畏戰，故月氏徙來，皆臣畜之，共稟漢使者。

translations.literal: Daxia originally lacked great lord chief; cities often set petty chiefs; people weak feared war; therefore Yuezhi migrated came; all made them subjects livestock; jointly supplied Han envoys.
translations.idiomatic: Daxia once had no paramount ruler—only petty town heads—and the people were timid in war. When the Yuezhi arrived they herded them like clients, yet together they provisioned Han envoys.

sentences.zh: 有五翕侯：一曰休密翕侯，治和墨城，去都護二千八百四十一里，去陽關七千八百二里；

translations.literal: Has five Xihou: first called Xiumi Xihou, rules Hemocheng, from Protector-General two thousand eight hundred forty-one li, from Yang Pass seven thousand eight hundred two li;
translations.idiomatic: Five vassal princes served the Yuezhi: first, the Xiumi prince at Hemo—2,841 li from the Protector-General and 7,802 li from Yang Pass;

sentences.zh: 二曰雙靡翕侯，治雙靡城，去都護三千七百四十一里，去陽關七千七百八十二里；

translations.literal: Second called Shuangmi Xihou, rules Shuangmi city, from Protector-General three thousand seven hundred forty-one li, from Yang Pass seven thousand seven hundred eighty-two li;
translations.idiomatic: second, the Shuangmi prince at Shuangmi—3,741 li from the Protector-General and 7,782 li from Yang Pass;

sentences.zh: 三曰貴霜翕侯，治護澡城，去都護五千九百四十里，去陽關七千九百八十二里，四曰肸頓翕侯，治薄茅城，去都護五千九百六十二里，去陽關八千二百二里；

translations.literal: Third called Guishuang Xihou, rules Huzao city, from Protector-General five thousand nine hundred forty li, from Yang Pass seven thousand nine hundred eighty-two li; fourth called Xidun Xihou, rules Bomao city, from Protector-General five thousand nine hundred sixty-two li, from Yang Pass eight thousand two hundred two li;
translations.idiomatic: third, the Guishuang prince at Huzao—5,940 li from the Protector-General and 7,982 li from Yang Pass; fourth, the Xidun prince at Bomao—5,962 li from the Protector-General and 8,202 li from Yang Pass;

sentences.zh: 五曰離附翕侯，治高附城，去都護六千四十一里，去陽關九千二百八十三里。

translations.literal: Fifth called Lifu Xihou, rules Gaofu city, from Protector-General six thousand forty-one li, from Yang Pass nine thousand two hundred eighty-three li.
translations.idiomatic: fifth, the Lifu prince at Gaofu—6,041 li from the Protector-General and 9,283 li from Yang Pass.

sentences.zh: 凡五翕侯，皆屬大月氏。

translations.literal: In total five Xihou—all belong Great Yuezhi.
translations.idiomatic: All five princes answered to the Great Yuezhi court.

sentences.zh: 康居國，王冬治樂越匿地。

translations.literal: Kangju state—king winter governs Leyueyi territory.
translations.idiomatic: The king of Kangju winters in the district of Leyueyi.

sentences.zh: 到卑闐城。

translations.literal: Reaches Beitian city.
translations.idiomatic: That seat reaches Beitian.

sentences.zh: 去長安萬二千三百里。

translations.literal: From Chang'an twelve thousand three hundred li.
translations.idiomatic: It lies twelve thousand three hundred li from Chang'an.

sentences.zh: 不屬都護。

translations.literal: Does not belong to Protector-General.
translations.idiomatic: Han's Protector-General did not govern it.

sentences.zh: 至越匿地馬行七日，至王夏所居蕃內九千一百四里。

translations.literal: Reach Yueyi territory horse journey seven days; reach king summer dwelling inner domain nine thousand one hundred four li.
translations.idiomatic: Seven days by horse from Yueyi brings one to the king's summer residence inside the inner marches, 9,104 li distant.

sentences.zh: 戶十二萬，口六十萬，勝兵十二萬人。

translations.literal: Households one hundred twenty thousand, mouths six hundred thousand, victors-in-arms one hundred twenty thousand persons.
translations.idiomatic: The realm counted 120,000 households, 600,000 people, and 120,000 soldiers.

sentences.zh: 東至都護治所五千五百五十里。

translations.literal: East to Protector-General governing seat five thousand five hundred fifty li.
translations.idiomatic: It stood 5,550 li west of the Protector-General's headquarters.

sentences.zh: 與大月氏同俗。

translations.literal: With Great Yuezhi same customs.
translations.idiomatic: Its customs matched those of the Great Yuezhi.

sentences.zh: 東羈事匈奴。

translations.literal: East tethered serve Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: To the east it remained tethered in service to the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 宣帝時，匈奴乖亂，五單于並爭，漢擁立呼韓邪單于，而郅支單于怨望，殺漢使者，西阻康居。

translations.literal: Emperor Xuandi time; Xiongnu rebellious chaos; five Chanyus together struggled; Han raised Huhanye Chanyu; while Zhizhi Chanyu resentful, killed Han envoys, west obstructed Kangju.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Xuandi the Xiongnu fractured into five rival Chanyus. Han enthroned Huhanye, while Zhizhi murdered Han envoys in resentment and fled west to shelter with Kangju.

sentences.zh: 其後都護甘延壽、副校尉陳湯發戊己校尉西域諸國兵至康居，誅滅郅支單于，語在《甘延壽、陳湯傳》。
